text: A 5 kg block accelerates from rest to 10 m/s over 2 seconds. The net force applied is:
explanation: a = Δv/Δt = 10/2 = 5 m/s². F = ma = 5 × 5 = 25 N. (Net force = 25 N in the direction of motion; any friction forces would be in addition to this net force.)

text: A 10 kg box sits on a surface with μs = 0.4 and μk = 0.3 (g = 10 m/s²). The minimum force to start it moving is:
explanation: Maximum static friction: fs_max = μs × N = 0.4 × (10 × 10) = 40 N. Any horizontal force less than 40 N will be balanced by static friction. The minimum force to start moving is just above 40 N. Once moving, kinetic friction = μk × N = 0.3 × 100 = 30 N (less than starting force).

text: A ball is dropped from 20 m height (g = 10 m/s²). The time to reach the ground is:
explanation: h = ½gt² → 20 = ½(10)t² → 20 = 5t² → t² = 4 → t = 2 s. Final speed: v = gt = 10 × 2 = 20 m/s (or v² = 2gh = 2×10×20 = 400 → v = 20 m/s). ✓

text: The slope of a velocity-time graph represents:
explanation: On a velocity-time (v-t) graph, the slope = Δv/Δt = acceleration. Area under the curve = displacement. For constant acceleration (straight line on v-t), slope = a = constant. For uniform motion (horizontal line), slope = 0 = zero acceleration.

text: An object is launched at 45° above horizontal at speed v₀. Its maximum range (horizontal distance) is:
explanation: Range R = v₀² sin(2θ)/g. Maximum range when sin(2θ) = 1 (θ = 45°): R_max = v₀²/g. At any angle θ, complementary angles (e.g., 30° and 60°) give equal range. Maximum height: H = v₀²sin²θ/(2g) = v₀²/(4g) at 45°.

text: Two objects of masses m and 4m are connected by a string over a frictionless pulley (one hangs freely; the other is on a frictionless horizontal surface). The acceleration is:
explanation: Hanging mass (4m) drives system; 4mg − T = 4m·a. Mass on surface: T = m·a. Adding: 4mg = 5m·a → a = 4g/5. Wait: hanging mass drives it: net force = 4mg (weight of hanging mass, if 4m hangs). a = 4mg/(m + 4m) = 4g/5. If m hangs and 4m is on surface: a = mg/5m = g/5.

text: A car moving at 20 m/s applies brakes with a = −5 m/s². The stopping distance is:
explanation: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx → 0 = (20)² + 2(−5)Δx → 0 = 400 − 10Δx → Δx = 40 m. Alternatively: t_stop = v₀/|a| = 20/5 = 4 s; Δx = v₀t + ½at² = 20(4) + ½(−5)(16) = 80 − 40 = 40 m. ✓

text: A roller coaster starts from rest at height h₁ = 30 m and reaches height h₂ = 10 m (g = 10 m/s²). Ignoring friction, its speed at h₂ is:
explanation: Energy conservation: mgh₁ = ½mv² + mgh₂ → v² = 2g(h₁−h₂) = 2(10)(30−10) = 400 → v = 20 m/s. The change in height of 20 m is what matters. (Note: 14.1 m/s would correspond to 10 m drop from rest; 10 m/s to 5 m drop.)

text: Which quantity is conserved in all collisions (elastic and inelastic)?
explanation: Momentum is always conserved in collisions (isolated system, no external forces) — it follows directly from Newton's 3rd Law. Kinetic energy is conserved only in elastic collisions. Total mechanical energy is not conserved if friction or inelastic deformation occurs.

text: The torque produced by a force F acting at distance r from the pivot at angle θ is:
explanation: Torque τ = r × F = rF sinθ (where θ is angle between r and F vectors). Maximum torque when F is perpendicular to r (θ = 90°): τ = rF. Zero torque when F is parallel to r. Units: N·m. For static equilibrium: Στ = 0 and ΣF = 0.

text: For a solid cylinder rolling down an incline, compared to a sphere rolling down the same incline:
options: The cylinder reaches the bottom faster
The sphere reaches the bottom faster (less rotational inertia)
explanation: For a sphere (I = 2/5 mr²), acceleration a_sphere = g sinθ/(1 + 2/5) = 5gsinθ/7. For cylinder (I = ½ mr²), a_cylinder = g sinθ/(1 + 1/2) = 2gsinθ/3. Since 5/7 > 2/3, the sphere accelerates faster and reaches the bottom first. Hollow cylinder has I = mr² (slowest).

text: A pump lifts water at flow rate Q = 0.02 m³/s through height h = 5 m (ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³, g = 10 m/s²). The minimum power of the pump is:
explanation: Power = rate of doing work against gravity = ρQgh = (1000)(0.02)(10)(5) = 1000 W = 1 kW. P = mgh/t = (ρ × Q × t × g × h)/t = ρQgh. This is the minimum ideal power (no friction losses in real pumps).

text: The rate of heat conduction through a wall is greatest when:
options: Wall thickness is large and ΔT is small
Wall thickness is small, thermal conductivity is high, and ΔT is large
Area is small and ΔT is large
Temperature is uniform throughout the wall
explanation: Fourier's Law: Q/t = kA(ΔT/L). To maximize heat flow: high k (thermal conductivity), large A (cross-section), large ΔT (temperature difference), small L (thin wall). Insulation works by having small k (e.g., fiberglass, air gaps) and increased effective L.

text: The first law of thermodynamics, ΔU = q + w, where w = −PΔV (work done BY the system). For an adiabatic process:
explanation: Adiabatic: no heat exchange with surroundings (q = 0). By first law: ΔU = 0 + w = w. For expansion against external pressure: w = −PΔV (work done by system is negative for expansion if convention w = work done ON system). Adiabatic expansion cools the gas (ΔU < 0, T decreases).

text: Hess's Law calculation: Given ΔH for 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O = −484 kJ, what is ΔH for H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂?
explanation: Reverse the original reaction AND divide by 2: ΔH for 2H₂O → 2H₂ + O₂ = +484 kJ (reverse = same magnitude, opposite sign). For H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂ (half the coefficients): ΔH = +484/2 = +242 kJ. Endothermic (makes sense: decomposing water requires energy input).

text: The standard enthalpy of formation of CO₂ (ΔHf° = −393.5 kJ/mol) means:
options: CO₂ has 393.5 kJ of internal energy
The reverse reaction has ΔH° = −393.5 kJ
explanation: ΔHf° is the enthalpy change for forming 1 mole of compound from its constituent elements in their standard states. C(graphite) + O₂(g) → CO₂(g), ΔH°f = −393.5 kJ/mol. This reaction is exothermic (combustion of carbon). Used with Hess's Law: ΔH°rxn = Σ ΔHf°(products) − Σ ΔHf°(reactants).

text: Bond enthalpy (bond energy) is used to estimate ΔH for a reaction. The equation is:
explanation: ΔH ≈ Σ D(bonds broken, reactants) − Σ D(bonds formed, products). Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic, +); forming bonds releases energy (exothermic, −). Net: if bonds formed are stronger than bonds broken → ΔH < 0 (exothermic overall). Average bond enthalpies give approximate answers.

text: Which molecule has the highest dipole moment?
explanation: Dipole moment depends on both bond polarity and molecular geometry. H₂O: O-H bonds are very polar (O is highly electronegative), and the bent geometry means the bond dipoles don't cancel → large net dipole moment (μ = 1.85 D). Symmetric molecules (CO₂, CCl₄, CH₄) have bond dipoles that cancel.

text: Electronegativity difference determines bond type: Δ EN > 2.0 = ionic; 0.5–2.0 = polar covalent; < 0.5 = nonpolar covalent. The C-F bond (EN: C=2.5, F=4.0, Δ EN=1.5) is:
explanation: Δ EN = 4.0 − 2.5 = 1.5, which falls in the polar covalent range (0.5 to 2.0). The C-F bond is strongly polar (F pulls electron density), making organofluorine compounds useful in pharmaceuticals (metabolic stability) and nonstick surfaces (PTFE/Teflon).

text: Which process in the nitrogen cycle converts N₂ gas into ammonia (NH₃)?
explanation: Nitrogen fixation: N₂(g) → NH₃ (done by nitrogen-fixing bacteria such as Rhizobium and Azotobacter, which have nitrogenase enzyme, or industrially by Haber-Bosch process). Nitrification: NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻. Denitrification: NO₃⁻ → N₂. Ammonification: organic N → NH₃.

text: A voltaic cell has E°cell = 1.10 V for the Zn/Cu cell: Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ (anode) and Cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Cu (cathode). The ΔG° for the reaction is (n = 2, F = 96,485 C/mol):
explanation: ΔG° = −nFE° = −(2)(96,485 C/mol)(1.10 V) = −212,267 J/mol ≈ −212 kJ/mol. The negative value confirms the reaction is spontaneous (as expected for a galvanic cell). This is the Daniel cell (1836), the classic galvanic cell.

text: Which is the correct hierarchy of acid strength for these carboxylic acids: acetic acid (CH₃COOH), chloroacetic acid (ClCH₂COOH), dichloroacetic acid (Cl₂CHCOOH)?
options: Cl₂CHCOOH > ClCH₂COOH > CH₃COOH (more Cl = stronger EWG = stronger acid)
explanation: Electronegative Cl atoms are electron-withdrawing groups (EWGs) that stabilize the conjugate base (carboxylate anion) by inductive effect, increasing acid strength. More Cl = stronger EWG = more stable carboxylate = stronger acid. pKa: acetic (4.74) > chloroacetic (2.86) > dichloroacetic (1.48). EWGs increase acid strength; EDGs decrease it.

text: The pH of a 0.10 M acetic acid solution (Ka = 1.8 × 10⁻⁵) is approximately:
explanation: CH₃COOH ⇌ H⁺ + CH₃COO⁻. Ka = x²/(0.10 − x) ≈ x²/0.10 (weak acid approx: x << 0.10). x² = Ka × 0.10 = 1.8 × 10⁻⁶. x = [H⁺] = 1.34 × 10⁻³ M. pH = −log(1.34 × 10⁻³) ≈ 2.87. Check: x/C = 1.34×10⁻³/0.10 = 1.34% << 5%, approximation valid.

text: In a galvanic cell with E°cell = 0.76 V (Zn/H₂ cell) and n = 2, the equilibrium constant K at 25°C is:
explanation: ΔG° = −RT ln K = −nFE°. So ln K = nFE°/(RT) = nE°/(0.02569 V) at 25°C; or log K = nE°/(0.0592 V) = 2(0.76)/0.0592 = 25.7. K = 10^25.7 ≈ 5 × 10^25. Extremely large K → essentially complete reaction at equilibrium.

text: The conductivity of a strong electrolyte solution compared to a weak electrolyte at equal concentration is:
options: Lower because strong electrolytes have larger ions
Higher because strong electrolytes fully dissociate → more ions to carry charge
Equal, since number of ions doesn't affect conductivity
Lower because the solution is too concentrated
explanation: Electrical conductivity of a solution depends on the number and mobility of ions. Strong electrolytes (e.g., NaCl, HCl) fully dissociate → maximum ion concentration → high conductivity. Weak electrolytes (e.g., acetic acid, NH₃) partially dissociate → fewer ions → lower conductivity. At infinite dilution, weak electrolytes approach full dissociation.

text: The percent ionization of a weak acid INCREASES as:
options: Concentration of weak acid decreases (Ka stays constant, so lower C → more dissociation fraction)
Conjugate base is added to the solution
explanation: For HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻: Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA] ≈ x²/(C−x). Percent ionization = (x/C) × 100%. Solving: x = √(Ka × C) (approximately), so x/C = √(Ka/C). As C decreases, x/C (percent ionization) increases. More dilute weak acid = greater percent dissociation (Le Chatelier: dilution shifts equilibrium right).

text: The reduction half-reaction for O₂ in acidic solution is: O₂ + 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ → 2H₂O, E° = +1.23 V. The oxidation half-reaction for H₂ is: H₂ → 2H⁺ + 2e⁻, E° = 0 V (by definition). The overall E° for the hydrogen fuel cell is:
explanation: Fuel cell: H₂ is oxidized at anode (E° = 0 V), O₂ is reduced at cathode (E° = +1.23 V). E°cell = E°cathode − E°anode = 1.23 − 0 = 1.23 V. Note: multiplying half-reactions to balance electrons does NOT change E° (intensive property). Overall: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, ΔG° = −nFE° = −4(96485)(1.23) = −474 kJ.

text: In which order do signals appear in ¹H NMR for the compound CH₃-C(=O)-CH₂-OH (hydroxyacetone)?
options: Three signals: CH₃ (δ~2.1), CH₂ (δ~4.3), OH (δ~3-5, variable)
Two signals (CH₃ and CH₂ are equivalent)
explanation: Hydroxyacetone has 3 types of H: (1) CH₃ next to carbonyl (δ~2.1, 3H, singlet — no adjacent H for coupling); (2) CH₂ next to carbonyl and OH (δ~4.3, 2H); (3) OH (broad singlet, variable δ, 1H). Three ¹H NMR signals total. Integration ratio = 3:2:1.

text: The melting point of a compound is a characteristic physical property used for identification. A sharp melting point indicates:
options: The compound is a pure substance (pure compounds melt sharply; impurities broaden and lower the melting range)
The compound has high molecular weight
The compound has strong covalent bonds only
explanation: Pure crystalline compounds have sharp, characteristic melting points (narrow range, <1°C). Impurities disrupt the crystal lattice, depressing and broadening the melting point. Mixture melting point depression is also used to confirm identity: mix known with unknown — if mp is unaffected, they're the same compound; if depressed, they're different.

text: Boiling point of organic compounds is influenced primarily by:
options: Intermolecular forces: London dispersion (all molecules), dipole-dipole (polar molecules), and hydrogen bonding (N-H, O-H, or F-H donors)
explanation: Boiling point hierarchy (same molecular weight): H-bonding compounds > polar (dipole-dipole) > nonpolar (London dispersion only). Among nonpolar compounds: higher MW or more elongated (greater surface area) → stronger London dispersion → higher bp. Branching decreases surface area → lower bp than unbranched isomer.

text: In electrophoresis, proteins migrate toward:
options: The cathode (negative electrode) if they carry net positive charge at the buffer pH
The anode (positive electrode) if they carry net positive charge
explanation: In electrophoresis, positive charges migrate toward the cathode (negative electrode) and negative charges toward the anode (positive electrode). At pH below the protein's pI (isoelectric point), protein carries net positive charge → migrates toward cathode. At pH above pI, net negative charge → migrates toward anode. At pH = pI, no migration.

text: The Bragg equation (nλ = 2d sinθ) describes:
options: Refraction of light through a prism
Diffraction of X-rays by crystal lattice planes (determines crystal structure)
Reflection of sound in a room
Emission of electrons from a metal surface
explanation: Bragg's Law: nλ = 2d sinθ, where n = integer, λ = X-ray wavelength, d = lattice plane spacing, θ = angle of incidence. Constructive interference of X-rays reflected from parallel crystal planes allows determination of d-spacings and crystal structure (X-ray crystallography). Used to determine structures of DNA (Watson/Crick/Franklin), proteins, and inorganic materials.

text: An α particle has:
explanation: Alpha particle = helium-4 nucleus = 2 protons + 2 neutrons. Symbol: ⁴He²⁺ or α. Charge = +2, mass number = 4. α decay: parent nucleus loses 4 in mass number and 2 in atomic number. E.g., ²³⁸U → ²³⁴Th + ⁴He (alpha). Alpha particles are stopped by paper or a few cm of air.

text: In gamma decay, the nucleus:
options: Emits a high-energy photon (γ ray) without changing Z or A
Gains an electron from an inner shell
explanation: Gamma decay: excited nucleus (indicated by * or m for metastable) releases a high-energy photon (γ ray, ≥ 100 keV) to reach lower energy state. Z and A are unchanged (no change in nuclear composition). Often follows alpha or beta decay (daughter in excited state). Example: ⁶⁰Co (used in cancer radiotherapy).

text: The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation. A phosphate buffer: H₂PO₄⁻ ⇌ H⁺ + HPO₄²⁻ (pKa = 7.2) at pH 7.4. The ratio [HPO₄²⁻]/[H₂PO₄⁻] is:
explanation: H-H: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]) → 7.4 = 7.2 + log([HPO₄²⁻]/[H₂PO₄⁻]) → log([HPO₄²⁻]/[H₂PO₄⁻]) = 0.2 → [HPO₄²⁻]/[H₂PO₄⁻] = 10^0.2 ≈ 1.58. At pH 7.4 (slightly above pKa 7.2), more conjugate base (HPO₄²⁻) than acid (H₂PO₄⁻). This is the physiological intracellular phosphate buffer.

text: Oxidation of a primary alcohol by acidic dichromate (K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄) gives:
options: A carboxylic acid (primary alcohols are over-oxidized to carboxylic acids by strong oxidants)
explanation: Strong oxidizing agents (K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄, KMnO₄) convert: primary alcohols → carboxylic acids (via aldehyde intermediate that doesn't accumulate under these conditions); secondary alcohols → ketones; tertiary alcohols → no oxidation (no α-H on the carbon bearing OH). Use PCC to stop at aldehyde stage.

text: What is the product of the reaction between HBr and propene (CH₃CH=CH₂) following Markovnikov's rule?
options: 2-bromopropane (Br on C2 — more substituted carbon, more stable carbocation at C2)
explanation: Markovnikov: H⁺ adds to C1 (more H atoms), forming a secondary carbocation at C2 (more stable than primary at C1). Br⁻ attacks C2 → 2-bromopropane (CH₃CHBrCH₃). Anti-Markovnikov (HBr + peroxides → radical mechanism): Br adds to C1 → 1-bromopropane.

text: The mechanism of nucleophilic addition to an aldehyde involves:
options: The nucleophile attacks the carbonyl oxygen
The nucleophile attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon (δ+), followed by protonation of the resulting alkoxide
Radical chain mechanism initiated by light
explanation: The carbonyl carbon is electrophilic (δ+) due to the electronegative oxygen. Nucleophile attacks carbonyl carbon → tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate → protonation → product. Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones (less steric hindrance, less electron-donating R groups). Examples: cyanohydrin formation (HCN + RCHO), hemiacetal (alcohol + RCHO).

text: Which of the following carboxylic acid derivatives is most susceptible to nucleophilic acyl substitution?
explanation: Reactivity order: acyl halide > anhydride > ester ≈ carboxylic acid > amide. An acid anhydride (RC(=O)O(O=)CR') has a moderately good leaving group (carboxylate, pKa ~5), making it more reactive than esters (pKa ~16 for alkoxide) but less reactive than acyl halides (halide, pKa ~−7 for HCl). Among the choices, anhydride > ester > carboxylic acid > amide.

text: Cyclohexane preferentially adopts the chair conformation because:
options: It eliminates angle strain and minimizes torsional and steric strain (compared to boat conformation)
Boat conformation is always more stable
explanation: Chair conformation: bond angles ~111° (close to ideal 109.5°), all bonds fully staggered (no torsional strain), large substituents prefer equatorial positions (less steric 1,3-diaxial strain). Boat conformation has flagpole H-H interactions (steric) and partial eclipsing (torsional strain). Equatorial is preferred over axial for large groups (e.g., methylcyclohexane: equatorial methyl ~95% at room temperature).

text: A thin film of soap (n=1.33) on water (n=1.33) in air reflects light. What thickness causes constructive interference for λ = 532 nm (green) light at normal incidence?
explanation: Thin film interference (air-film-water): light reflects at air-film interface (low n to high n → phase flip) and film-water interface (no phase flip if n_film > n_water, but here they're equal — actually both interfaces have same n so: one phase flip total). Constructive interference condition with one flip: 2nt = (m + ½)λ_vacuum. For m = 0: t = λ/(4n) = 532/(4×1.33) ≈ 100 nm. For m = 1: t = 3λ/(4n) ≈ 300 nm. Option B (200 nm) corresponds to destructive (m=0: 2nt = mλ → t = mλ/2n = 532/2.66 ≈ 200 nm for destructive). Actually the exact answer depends on phase conditions; this tests the 2nt relationship.

text: Young's double-slit experiment produces:
options: A single bright spot from diffraction
An alternating bright and dark interference pattern because light from the two slits interferes constructively (bright) and destructively (dark)
A single dark spot in the middle
explanation: Young's double-slit: coherent light through two slits → two coherent sources that interfere. Bright fringes (constructive): path difference = mλ. Dark fringes (destructive): path difference = (m + ½)λ. Fringe spacing y = λL/d (L = screen distance, d = slit separation). This experiment definitively proved wave nature of light (Newton's corpuscular theory predicted two bright spots).

text: Which expression gives the energy of a photon given its frequency f?
options: E = mc² (rest energy of a photon)
explanation: Planck's quantum hypothesis: E = hf = hc/λ, where h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s (Planck's constant). A photon of red light (λ = 700 nm, f = 4.3 × 10¹⁴ Hz): E = (6.626×10⁻³⁴)(4.3×10¹⁴) ≈ 2.8 × 10⁻¹⁹ J ≈ 1.77 eV. Blue light (λ = 400 nm): E ≈ 3.1 eV.

text: The specific heat capacity of a substance is a measure of:
options: The total heat energy stored in the substance
The energy required to raise the temperature of 1 gram of the substance by 1°C (or 1 K)
The energy released during a phase transition
The energy per mole at absolute zero
explanation: Specific heat (c) = energy per gram per degree. For water: c = 4.18 J/(g·°C) — unusually high due to hydrogen bonding. Metals have lower specific heats (~0.2–0.9 J/g·°C). q = mcΔT. Molar heat capacity C = M × c (molar mass × specific heat). Heat capacity (C_p) at constant pressure for an ideal monatomic gas = 5/2 R.

text: Entropy change for vaporization of water (ΔSvap) is:
options: Positive (ΔSvap = ΔHvap/T_boil > 0, as liquid → gas greatly increases disorder)
explanation: Vaporization: liquid → gas. Entropy increases dramatically (gas molecules have far more translational/rotational freedom than liquid). ΔSvap = ΔHvap/T_boil = 40,700 J/(373 K) ≈ 109 J/(mol·K) for water at 100°C. Trouton's rule: many liquids have ΔSvap ≈ 85–90 J/(mol·K) (reflects similar entropy increase per mole on vaporization).

text: A gas is compressed isothermally (constant temperature). The change in internal energy of an ideal gas is:
options: ΔU = nCvΔT = 0 (since ΔT = 0 for ideal gas)
explanation: For an ideal gas, U depends only on temperature. Isothermal process: ΔT = 0 → ΔU = 0. By first law: 0 = q + w → q = −w. Work done ON the gas during compression (w > 0) means heat flows OUT (q < 0) to maintain constant T. In an isothermal compression in contact with a heat reservoir, the internal energy does not change.

text: What happens to the rate constant k when a catalyst is added at constant temperature?
options: k decreases because the activation energy decreases
k increases because the activation energy decreases (Arrhenius: k = Ae^(−Ea/RT); lower Ea → larger k)
k is unchanged; only the equilibrium constant changes
k decreases because the enthalpy of reaction decreases
explanation: A catalyst lowers activation energy Ea. By Arrhenius equation: k = Ae^(−Ea/RT). Lower Ea → e^(−Ea/RT) is larger → k is larger (rate constant increases). Catalyst increases rate of BOTH forward and reverse reactions proportionally, so K is unchanged. ΔG, ΔH, ΔS are also unchanged by a catalyst.

text: For the reaction 2NO₂(g) → 2NO(g) + O₂(g), the rate law is rate = k[NO₂]². If the concentration of NO₂ is tripled:
explanation: Rate = k[NO₂]². If [NO₂] → 3[NO₂]: Rate_new = k(3[NO₂])² = 9k[NO₂]² = 9 × Rate_old. Rate increases by factor 9. For a second-order reaction, rate is proportional to concentration squared; tripling concentration increases rate by 3² = 9.

text: Which statement correctly describes a catalyst and the energy diagram for a reaction?
options: Catalyst lowers the energy of the reactants
Catalyst raises the energy of the products
Catalyst lowers the activation energy (Ea) by providing an alternative pathway with a different (lower) transition state energy
Catalyst shifts the equilibrium toward products by changing ΔG°
explanation: On an energy diagram, a catalyst creates a new reaction pathway with lower Ea (lower transition state energy). The reactant and product energies are unchanged (ΔH and ΔG° unchanged). The new path has a lower peak, allowing more molecules to have sufficient energy to react (Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution). The catalyst is regenerated.

text: Henry's Law: a diver at 40 m depth breathes compressed air at 5 atm. The partial pressure of N₂ at depth is approximately:
explanation: Air is ~79% N₂. At 40 m: total pressure ≈ 5 atm (1 atm surface + 4 atm water = 5 atm). P(N₂) = 0.79 × 5 = 3.95 atm. By Henry's Law: [N₂ dissolved] ∝ P(N₂) = 3.95 atm. This is ~5× more N₂ dissolved than at surface — causes nitrogen narcosis and decompression sickness on ascent.

text: For a rigid container (constant volume, isochoric) of ideal gas heated from 300 K to 600 K:
options: Pressure doubles (P ∝ T at constant V and n)
explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law: P/T = constant at constant V and n. P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂ → P₂ = P₁(T₂/T₁) = P₁(600/300) = 2P₁. Pressure doubles when absolute temperature doubles at constant volume. For an ideal gas in a rigid container, temperature directly controls pressure.

text: The Clapeyron equation (dP/dT = ΔS/ΔV = ΔH/(TΔV)) describes:
options: Rate of chemical reaction as a function of temperature
The slope of phase boundaries in a phase diagram
Change in entropy during a chemical reaction
Vapor pressure as a function of concentration
explanation: The Clapeyron equation gives the slope of phase boundaries (solid-liquid, liquid-gas, solid-gas lines) in the P-T phase diagram: dP/dT = ΔHtransition/(T × ΔV). For the solid-liquid boundary of most substances (ΔV > 0): slope is positive. For water ice: ΔV < 0 (water contracts on melting) → negative slope (pressure lowers melting point of ice).

text: Avogadro's hypothesis states that equal volumes of different gases at the same T and P contain:
options: Equal numbers of molecules (Avogadro's number of molecules per mole)
The same number of atoms only if monatomic
explanation: Avogadro's Law: equal volumes of ideal gases at the same T and P contain the same number of molecules (regardless of the gas's identity). This is implicit in the ideal gas law: n = PV/RT. At STP (0°C, 1 atm): 1 mole of any ideal gas = 22.4 L. Avogadro's number: 6.022 × 10²³ molecules/mol.

text: In the electromagnetic spectrum, which type of radiation has the longest wavelength?
explanation: EM spectrum (longest to shortest wavelength, lowest to highest frequency/energy): radio > microwave > infrared > visible > ultraviolet > X-ray > gamma. Radio waves: λ from km to m. Gamma rays: λ < 0.01 nm. Lower frequency = lower energy (E = hf). Radio waves carry the least energy per photon; gamma rays the most.

text: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is based on:
options: Radiofrequency emission by hydrogen nuclei (¹H) in a magnetic field after being excited by RF pulses (NMR principle)
Gamma ray emission by radioactive isotopes
Sound wave reflection from tissue boundaries
explanation: MRI uses NMR (nuclear magnetic resonance) of ¹H (protons) in water and fat. A strong external magnetic field aligns ¹H spins; RF pulses at the Larmor frequency excite them; relaxation emits RF signals detected to create images. Different tissues have different T1 and T2 relaxation times → tissue contrast. No ionizing radiation (unlike CT/X-ray).

text: The angular momentum of an electron in a Bohr orbit is quantized: L = nℏ. For the n=2 orbit, the angular momentum is:
explanation: In the Bohr model, allowed angular momentum values are L = nℏ where n = 1, 2, 3, ... and ℏ = h/(2π). For n=2: L = 2ℏ. This quantization was Bohr's key postulate and was later justified by de Broglie's wave nature of the electron (standing wave condition: nλ = 2πr → L = nℏ).

text: Which combination of quantum numbers is NOT allowed?
options: n=2, l=2, ml=0, ms=+½ (l must be < n)
explanation: For quantum numbers: l must range from 0 to (n−1). For n=2: l can only be 0 or 1; l=2 is NOT allowed. ml ranges from −l to +l (for l=2: ml = −2,−1,0,+1,+2). ms = ±½ always. Check: option C has n=2, l=2 → invalid since l must be ≤ n−1 = 1.

text: Positron emission tomography (PET) uses:
options: Positrons (β⁺ particles) emitted by radioactive nuclides (e.g., ¹⁸F); positrons annihilate with electrons → two 511 keV gamma photons detected coincidentally
Sound waves for deep tissue imaging
explanation: PET: a β⁺ emitter (¹⁸F in FDG = fluorodeoxyglucose) is injected. The positron quickly annihilates with a nearby electron → 2 gamma photons emitted in opposite directions (511 keV each). Coincidence detection of these gamma pairs localizes the annihilation. FDG accumulates in high-glucose-using tissues (tumors, brain, heart), enabling metabolic imaging.

text: An object of density 600 kg/m³ floats in water (density 1000 kg/m³). What fraction of the object is submerged?
explanation: For a floating object: buoyant force = weight → ρ_water × V_sub × g = ρ_obj × V_total × g. V_sub/V_total = ρ_obj/ρ_water = 600/1000 = 0.60. 60% of the object is submerged. (A wooden block of density 0.6 g/cm³ in water has 40% above the waterline.) If ρ_obj > ρ_water: object sinks completely (V_sub/V_total > 1, impossible, so sinks).

text: The Poiseuille equation for flow rate through a tube is Q = πr⁴ΔP/(8ηL). If the tube radius is doubled (all else constant):
explanation: Q ∝ r⁴. Doubling r: Q_new = π(2r)⁴ΔP/(8ηL) = 16πr⁴ΔP/(8ηL) = 16Q. Flow rate increases by factor 16. This strong r⁴ dependence explains why small decreases in arterial radius (from atherosclerotic plaque) dramatically reduce blood flow (and why vasodilation/vasoconstriction potently regulate blood flow).

text: Rutherford's gold foil experiment revealed:
options: Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels
The atom is a uniform 'plum pudding' of positive and negative charge
Most of the atom is empty space with a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus
The electron has a mass equal to the proton
explanation: Rutherford (1911): fired alpha particles at gold foil. Most passed through (atom is mostly empty space), but some deflected at large angles (small, dense nucleus). This disproved Thomson's plum pudding model and established the nuclear model. Geiger and Marsden performed the experiments.

text: Pair production (gamma ray → electron + positron) requires photon energy of at least:
options: 511 keV (rest mass energy of one electron/positron)
1.022 MeV (twice the electron rest mass energy, since TWO particles are created)
explanation: Pair production: γ → e⁻ + e⁺. By E = mc²: rest energy of each electron or positron = 0.511 MeV. Creating a pair requires at least 2 × 0.511 = 1.022 MeV of photon energy. Any excess energy becomes kinetic energy of the electron/positron. Occurs in the vicinity of a nucleus (to conserve momentum).

text: On a titration curve for a diprotic acid (H₂A), there are:
options: One equivalence point and one half-equivalence point
Two equivalence points (at half and full neutralization) and two buffer regions (each at a half-equivalence point where pH ≈ pKa1 or pKa2)
explanation: Diprotic acid titration: 1st equivalence point: H₂A + OH⁻ → HA⁻ (half of H₂A neutralized). 2nd equivalence point: HA⁻ + OH⁻ → A²⁻ (all neutralized). Two buffer regions: around pH = pKa1 (between 0 and 1st equiv.) and pH = pKa2 (between 1st and 2nd equiv.). Example: H₂CO₃ (pKa1=6.35, pKa2=10.33).

text: If the wavelength of light doubles, the energy of each photon:
explanation: E = hc/λ. If λ doubles: E_new = hc/(2λ) = E/2. Energy is inversely proportional to wavelength. Doubling λ (lower frequency light, more red-shifted) → half the photon energy. This explains why UV light causes more photodamage than infrared: higher energy per photon.

text: The principal quantum number n determines:
options: The orientation of the orbital in space
The energy level and average distance of the electron from the nucleus
explanation: Principal quantum number n (1, 2, 3...): determines the primary energy level (shell) and average electron-nucleus distance (r ∝ n² in Bohr model). n also sets the range of allowed l values (l = 0 to n−1) and thus the number of subshells and orbitals in each shell: n² orbitals per shell, 2n² electrons maximum.

text: Which type of emission line spectrum results from electrons falling from higher to lower energy levels?
options: Emission spectrum (bright lines at specific wavelengths corresponding to ΔE = hf)
explanation: Emission spectrum: when electrons fall from higher to lower energy levels, they emit photons with E = hf = ΔE. Each element has a unique set of energy levels → unique emission spectrum (fingerprint). For hydrogen: Lyman series (to n=1, UV), Balmer series (to n=2, visible), Paschen series (to n=3, IR).

text: The root-mean-square (rms) speed of gas molecules is given by:
options: v_rms = √(3RT/M) where M is molar mass in kg/mol
v_rms = √(kT/m) where k is the spring constant
explanation: v_rms = √(3RT/M), where R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), T = absolute temperature, M = molar mass in kg/mol. Average KE per molecule = ½mv_rms² = 3/2 kT (kinetic theory). At 300K, v_rms(H₂) = √(3×8.314×300/0.002) ≈ 1934 m/s; v_rms(O₂) = √(3×8.314×300/0.032) ≈ 484 m/s. Lighter gases move faster.

text: A ball on a string moves in a vertical circle. At the top of the circle, the minimum speed required to maintain circular motion is:
options: v = 0 (gravity always provides enough centripetal force)
v = √(rg), where the centripetal force is provided entirely by gravity (T = 0 at minimum speed)
explanation: At the top of a vertical circle, both gravity and tension point downward (toward center). At minimum speed, tension T = 0 and gravity alone provides centripetal force: mg = mv²/r → v_min = √(rg). If v < √(rg), the string would need to push (tension becomes negative), which a string can't do — it would go slack and the ball leaves circular motion.

text: Which of the following correctly applies Newton's Third Law?
options: A book on a table is in equilibrium because the table's normal force equals the book's weight (these are NOT Newton's 3rd Law pairs)
The friction force from the road on a car's tires (forward) and the friction force from the car's tires on the road (backward) are Newton's 3rd Law pairs
Gravitational force on a planet and centripetal force on the planet are Newton's 3rd Law pairs
The normal force and gravity on a book are Newton's 3rd Law pairs
explanation: Newton's 3rd Law: for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. The pairs must be: same type of force, between the same two objects, in opposite directions. The road exerts forward friction on the tire (what moves the car); the tire exerts backward friction on the road. These are a valid 3rd Law pair. The normal force and gravity on the book are NOT a 3rd Law pair — they're both on the same object and are different types of forces.

text: A block is pushed up a frictionless incline of angle θ with constant force F parallel to the incline. The work done by gravity over displacement d along the incline is:
options: W = −mgd sinθ (negative — gravity opposes uphill motion)
W = 0 (gravity is perpendicular to incline surface)
explanation: Gravity acts downward (magnitude mg). Displacement is along the incline (at angle θ to horizontal). Gravity component along the incline (opposing motion) = mg sinθ. Work by gravity: W_grav = −(mg sinθ)(d) = −mgd sinθ. Negative because force component (mg sinθ, down the incline) opposes displacement (up the incline). Equivalently: W_grav = −ΔPE = −mgh = −mg(d sinθ).

text: A wave has speed v = 340 m/s and frequency f = 1000 Hz. Its wavelength and period are:
explanation: λ = v/f = 340/1000 = 0.34 m. T = 1/f = 1/1000 = 0.001 s = 1 ms. These represent a 1000 Hz (1 kHz) sound wave in air. The wave travels 0.34 m (34 cm) per cycle and completes 1000 cycles per second.

text: Power transmitted by a wave is proportional to:
explanation: Wave power (energy per unit time) is proportional to the square of the amplitude: P ∝ A². This applies to all wave types (mechanical, EM). Intensity (power per unit area) I ∝ A². For a sound wave, doubling amplitude quadruples intensity (increases by 6 dB). For a photon: E = hf (one photon's energy); for many photons, total intensity ∝ n × E ∝ amplitude².

text: The decibel (dB) sound intensity level scale is logarithmic. A 10 dB increase corresponds to:
explanation: β = 10 log(I/I₀), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m² (threshold of hearing). Δβ = 10 log(I₂/I₁). If Δβ = 10 dB: 10 = 10 log(I₂/I₁) → log(I₂/I₁) = 1 → I₂/I₁ = 10. Every 10 dB = 10× intensity. Every 3 dB ≈ 2× intensity (since log₁₀(2) ≈ 0.3). A conversation at 60 dB is 10⁶× more intense than threshold.

text: Two equal charges of +q are placed 1 m apart. The electric force on each charge is F. If the distance is reduced to 0.5 m, the new force is:
explanation: Coulomb's Law: F = kq²/r². If r → r/2: F_new = kq²/(r/2)² = kq²/(r²/4) = 4kq²/r² = 4F. Halving the distance quadruples the force. The inverse square law means electrostatic (and gravitational) forces are extremely sensitive to distance.

text: A proton (charge +e) is placed in a uniform electric field E = 500 N/C. Its acceleration is (m_proton = 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg, e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C):
explanation: F = qE = (1.6×10⁻¹⁹)(500) = 8×10⁻¹⁷ N. a = F/m = 8×10⁻¹⁷/1.67×10⁻²⁷ ≈ 4.8×10¹⁰ m/s². Protons (small mass, fundamental charge) accelerate enormously in electric fields. Electrons (1836× lighter) accelerate even faster in the same field.

text: In a series RLC circuit at resonance:
options: The capacitive and inductive reactances cancel (X_L = X_C), impedance = R (minimum), current is maximum
All voltage appears across the resistor only
explanation: At resonance in a series RLC circuit: X_L = ωL = 1/(ωC) = X_C → impedances cancel → Z = R (minimum impedance). Maximum current I = V/R. Resonant frequency: ω₀ = 1/√(LC), f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)). Applications: radio tuning, MRI (Larmor frequency matching), filters.

text: Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) states:
options: The sum of voltages around any closed loop = 0
The sum of currents entering a node equals the sum of currents leaving (ΣI_in = ΣI_out)
Current is the same everywhere in a series circuit
Resistance divides proportionally among parallel resistors
explanation: KCL (junction rule): at any node (junction), ΣI_in = ΣI_out (conservation of charge). Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): around any closed loop, ΣΔV = 0 (conservation of energy). These two laws, combined with Ohm's Law, allow complete circuit analysis.

text: A transformer has primary voltage 240 V, secondary voltage 12 V. The turns ratio N_p/N_s is:
explanation: V_p/V_s = N_p/N_s. 240/12 = 20. N_p/N_s = 20 (step-down transformer: primary has 20× more turns than secondary, reduces voltage by 20). Secondary current is proportionally larger: I_s = I_p × (N_p/N_s) = 20 × I_p (more current at lower voltage to conserve power P = VI).

text: The displacement current (Ampere-Maxwell Law extension) is associated with:
options: Current through a capacitor's dielectric gap (no actual charge movement, but changing electric field produces a magnetic field as if current flowed)
explanation: Maxwell added a displacement current term to Ampere's Law: J_d = ε₀(∂E/∂t). A changing electric field (e.g., between capacitor plates as capacitor charges) creates a magnetic field, exactly as a real current would. This completed the symmetry of Maxwell's equations and predicts electromagnetic waves traveling at speed c = 1/√(ε₀μ₀).

text: An inductor L stores energy in its magnetic field when current I flows through it. The stored energy is:
explanation: Inductor energy: U = ½LI², where L = inductance (henries, H) and I = current (amperes). Compare to capacitor: U = ½CV². Inductors oppose changes in current (V = −L dI/dt) and store energy in magnetic fields; capacitors oppose changes in voltage and store energy in electric fields.

text: Which property of water is most directly responsible for the high heat capacity of oceans and moderation of coastal climates?
options: Water's high specific heat (4.18 J/g°C) due to extensive hydrogen bonding
explanation: Water's high specific heat (4.18 J/g°C, far higher than metals or air) means oceans can absorb vast amounts of solar energy with minimal temperature change, and release that energy slowly in winter. This moderates coastal climate (maritime climate: less extreme temperature swings than continental interiors). Both the absorption and release involve disrupting and forming hydrogen bonds.

text: A 2 μF capacitor is charged to 100 V. The charge stored is:
Two capacitors C₁ = 4 μF and C₂ = 4 μF connected in series. Their equivalent capacitance is:
explanation: Capacitors in series: 1/C_eq = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂ = 1/4 + 1/4 = 2/4 → C_eq = 2 μF. For two equal capacitors in series: C_eq = C/2. Note: for capacitors, series = smaller capacitance (like parallel for resistors); parallel = larger capacitance (like series for resistors). This is opposite to resistors.

text: The work done by an external agent to move charge Q through a potential difference ΔV is:
explanation: Work done to move charge: W = QΔV. This is equivalent to saying that potential difference is defined as V = W/Q (work per unit charge). When an electron (q = −e) moves through ΔV = +1 V (against the field), the work done ON the electron by external agent = (−e)(+1 V) = −1.6×10⁻¹⁹ J (field does negative work, external agent does positive work).
